questions.prompt: Why is `theme: { extend: { ... } }` almost always right?

choices.text: `extend` ADDS to Tailwind's defaults. `theme: { colors: {...} }` (without extend) REPLACES defaults entirely — you lose `gray`, `red`, `green`, all of Tailwind's well-tuned palette. The defaults are excellent and battle-tested; you want your brand colors on top, not instead. Use extend for nearly everything (colors, spacing, fontFamily, screens). Only replace when you have a deliberate reason — e.g., a brand with one mandated color palette and no need for Tailwind defaults at all.

questions.explanation: extend adds; bare theme replaces. You want your tokens alongside Tailwind's defaults, not instead.

questions.prompt: Brand has one blue color hardcoded as `#3496ff`. Designers ask for a darker hover and lighter disabled state. Approach?

choices.text: Generate a full 10-step palette (50-950) from the base color using a tool like uicolors.app. Add to tailwind.config under `extend.colors.brand`. Now `bg-brand-500` (the original), `bg-brand-600` (hover, slightly darker), `bg-brand-200` (disabled, lighter) all work consistently. Future requests for 'a bit more contrast' or 'a softer variant' have answers from the same palette. Defining once gives you expressive room without inventing one-off hex codes everywhere — and the consistency compounds across the design system.

questions.explanation: Generate a 50-950 scale from the base color. Express hover, disabled, etc. from the consistent palette.
